title: Complex-gauge control of anomalous Floquet corner responses in a non-Hermitian physical-synthetic photonic lattice
authors: W. C. Ning, X. Z. Zhang
abstract: We propose a non-Hermitian Floquet photonic lattice formed by a physical resonator coordinate and a synthetic frequency coordinate. A two-step modulation protocol realizes a chiral walk in this physical-synthetic plane, with a real synthetic flux controlling loop interference and imaginary gauge fields controlling non-reciprocal envelopes. We show that anomalous corner pairs at quasienergies zero and \(π/T\) exhibit three distinct layers of physics. A non-Bloch higher-order construction predicts whether the \(0/π\) corner pair exists under open boundaries. The imaginary gauge fields select where the right eigenmodes accumulate. The real flux controls the local interference matrix element that determines whether the doubled-period optical response is visible. As a result, the same topological coexistence sector can be bright, skin-dark, or flux-dark in a local optical measurement. We further show that the complex gauge can tune an exceptional point of the two-period corner propagator. At this point the anomalous response keeps its doubled-period sign alternation, but its envelope becomes algebraic because of a Jordan block. These results provide a photonic route to separate topological existence, skin-selected localization, optical visibility, and defective two-period dynamics in a non-Hermitian synthetic dimension.
summary: The research investigates anomalous Floquet corner responses in a complex non-Hermitian photonic lattice by coupling physical and synthetic dimensions. It rigorously separates the concept of topological existence (predicted by winding numbers) from actual local optical visibility, showing three possible measurement regimes. Furthermore, it details how tuning parameters can induce an exceptional point, causing the response to adopt a unique algebraic envelope structure.
main_problem: The study aims to decouple topological existence from local optical visibility of anomalous Floquet corner responses in a non-Hermitian photonic lattice.
main_result: The paper demonstrates that the same topological sector can exhibit three distinct measurement regimes (bright, skin-dark, or flux-dark), and furthermore, an exceptional point causes the response to acquire an algebraic envelope rather than simply being absent.
method: Theoretical analysis involving Floquet theory, non-Bloch winding number calculations, and constructing effective two-period propagators for corner subspace dynamics.
model_or_system: A non-Hermitian Floquet photonic lattice incorporating both a physical resonator coordinate and a synthetic frequency dimension. The system is controlled by complex gauge fields (real flux $\Phi$ and imaginary gauges $\gamma_{x,w}$).
key_observables: Right-state corner visibility amplitude ($S_{RR}^C$), non-Bloch winding number ($N_{	ext{corner}}$), local mode weights ($W^{(\epsilon)}_C$), and the stroboscopic signal $A_{RR}^{2T}(C)$.
important_parameters: Real synthetic magnetic flux ($\Phi$), imaginary gauge fields ($\gamma_x, \gamma_w$), and parameters governing the exceptional point ($\Delta_{	ext{EP}}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on projecting dynamics onto a corner subspace and analyzing the effective two-period propagator matrix.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate lattice structures, generalized Brillouin zone contours showing winding numbers, and time-domain traces comparing bright, skin-dark, and flux-dark regimes, culminating in visualizations of defective vs. diagonalizable responses near an exceptional point.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the physical model and its Hamiltonian structure to calculating topological invariants (winding numbers), then analyzing local measurement observables ($S_{RR}^C$), establishing the decoupling between topology and visibility, and finally investigating the role of exceptional points in modifying the time-domain response envelope.
why_interesting: This work provides a sophisticated theoretical framework for understanding how non-Hermiticity, synthetic dimensions, and topological invariants govern measurable optical responses, offering concrete predictions about mode localization and spectral signatures that are distinct from simple existence criteria.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Concept of the photonic physical-synthetic Floquet lattice and the two-step complex-gauge drive. Panel (a) shows the mode-space lattice. The horizontal direction is the physical resonator coordinate x, the vertical direction is the synthetic frequency coordinate w, and each site contains two internal optical modes A and B. The four corner windows CLD, CLU, CRD, and CRU are the local detection regions used for the doubled-period signal. Panel (b) shows the first half step, which implements a non-reciprocal synthetic-frequency drift proportional to σz; the upward and downward frequency-conversion amplitudes contain e+γw+iϕw and e−γw−iϕw, respectively. Panel (c) shows the second half...

FIG. 2. Flux-resolved non-Bloch higher-order invariant. Panel (a) shows representative generalized Brillouin-zone contours for different γx, together with the ordinary unit circle as a dashed reference. Panel (b) shows the first-layer physical-edge index eνx 0 . Panel (c) shows the corresponding index eνx π. Panel (d) shows the corner index N corner 0 after including the synthetic-edge contribution. Panel (e) shows N corner π . Panel (f) shows the coexistence region N corner 0 N corner π = 1. These maps are topological predictions obtained from non-Bloch cycles, not from finite open-boundary diagonalization. Figure 3 tests these predictions by finite open-boundary spectra and shows the...

FIG. 3. Open-boundary spectrum and right-eigenmode profiles. Panel (a) shows the finite open-boundary spectrum in the complex multiplier plane. Gray points are bulk states. Blue and red markers identify corner candidates near λ = +1 and λ = −1. Panel (b) shows log10(|ψR 0 (x, w)|2 + ϵnum) in the bright regime. Panel (c) shows the corresponding π mode. Here ϵnum = 10−8. Panel (d) gives the four corner weights and confirms that the two modes occupy the same lower-left window. Panel (e) shows the zero mode at a skin-dark point. Panel (f) shows the π mode at the same point. Both modes still exist, but the imaginary gauge fields select different synthetic corners for their right eigenmodes. This...

FIG. 4. Synthetic-flux and skin control of the doubled-period optical visibility. Panels (a)–(d) show ARR 2T (C) for CLD, CLU, CRD, and CRU, respectively, using a common color scale. Panels (e) and (f) compare discrete stroboscopic traces in the bright, skin-dark, flux-dark, and near-dark regimes. Panel (g) summarizes the extracted visibility at representative points. Panel (h) compares the corresponding local zero and π weights. Local weights are necessary but not sufficient for visibility. Flux darkness comes from interference cancellation, whereas skin darkness comes from spatial separation. Topological coexistence therefore does not imply local visibility.

FIG. 5. Flux-tuned exceptional point and defective doubled-period response. Panel (a) shows log10 |∆EP|, where ∆EP = δ2 + gh. The white marker identifies the exceptional point. Panel (b) shows the two-period multiplier splitting and the phase rigidity across the exceptional point. Their simultaneous collapse diagnoses eigenvalue and eigenvector coalescence. Panel (c) compares bounded and beating responses away from the exceptional point. Panel (d) shows the defective response and its algebraic envelope at the exceptional point. The exceptional point is defined in the projected two-period corner propagator, not in the one-period 0/π spectrum. It does not create the 2T signal. It dresses an...
